story.commons_artist: United States. Forest Service. Rocky Mountain Region
story.description: A clip of nocturnal animals: close up video of hanging paper bats
story.hook: Start with the first move; it explains the next move
story.lead: Bats hang upside down—and here’s the part nobody tells you: it’s not just for sleeping. Watch their tiny claws grip even when they’re dead still. That’s because hanging keeps them safe from predators on the ground, and it takes almost no energy to let gravity do the work. That’s why you’ll never see a bat nap on a branch. Ever wonder how they wake up so fast?
story.script: Bats hang upside down—and here’s the part nobody tells you: it’s not just for sleeping. Watch their tiny claws grip even when they’re dead still. That’s because hanging keeps them safe from predators on the ground, and it takes almost no energy to let gravity do the work. That’s why you’ll never see a bat nap on a branch. Ever wonder how they wake up so fast?
